rulings.Case Title: KRISTINA NENAYDOKH VS. GREG SHENKMAN et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For An Order Compelling Judgment Debtor Greg Shenkman To Provide Responses To Post-Judgment Written Discovery And For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, March 6, 2019, Line 3 - PLAINTIFF KRISTINA NENAYDOKH's Motion For An Order Compelling Judgment Debtor Greg Shenkman To Provide Responses To Post-Judgment Written Discovery And For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Vestal, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Mr. Shenkman is ordered to respond without objections to the Interrogatories and the Document Demands within 30 days of the entry of an order on the Motion. The request to turn over information and documents previously provided to Foreststream Holdings Ltd. is denied at this time. Sanctions are awarded against Mr. Shenkman in the amount of $2,194.95. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to PVestal@nvlawllp.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(JPT/302)

rulings.Case Title: REINA CRUZ CANTE VS. GERALDINE ANNE SCOTT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order To Seal Record
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, March 6, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT GERRIE SCOTT'S Motion For Protective Order To Seal Record Defendant Gerrie Scott's motion for a protective order to seal the record is off calendar. The motion was served by mail on February 14, 2019, and filed on February 19, 2019, with the supporting declaration filed on February 20, 2019. A motion must be served and filed at least 16 court days before the hearing. (Code Civ. Proc. § 1005(b).) For Defendant's guidance, the Court may order that a record be filed under seal only if it expressly finds facts that establish that there exists an overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record, the overriding interest supports sealing the record, a substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed, the proposed sealing is narrowly tailored, and no less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest that overcomes the right of public access to the record. (Cal. R. Ct. 2.550(d).) A court may not permit a record to be filed under seal based solely on the parties' stipulation or agreement. (Cal. R. Ct. 2.551(a).) A litigant's mere desire to avoid embarrassment or adverse publicity does not constitute an overriding interest. Further, the Court is exceedingly unlikely to grant a request seeking to seal an entire court file, which appears to be the relief sought by the motion ("any and all filings in this action"). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, March 6, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees (begining of tentative part 1 of 2) Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P.'s motion for attorney's fees is granted in the amount of $231,834.00. The attorneys' fees clause in the underlying construction contract between the parties stated, "If either party commences an action against the other to enforce any of the terms of the Contract Documents or because of the breach by either party of any of the terms of the Contract Documents, the losing or defaulting party shall pay to the prevailing party the actual costs and expenses incurred in connection with the prosecution or defense of such action and any appeals in connection therewith, including actual attorneys' fees, expert fees, consultant's fees or costs and compensation for their time spent by in-house costs." CDC's contention that the attorneys' fees clause is limited solely to "a breach of contract or contract enforcement action" (Opp. at 7) is belied by the broad language of that clause and by controlling case authority. "[A] contractual provision may provide that the party who prevails in litigation over noncontractual claims shall recover its attorney fees." (Maynard v. BTI Group, Inc. (2013) 216 Cal.App.4th 984, 990.) Civil Code section 1717, upon which CDC relies, is inapplicable in that situation, because it applies only to a contractual agreement that attorney fees may be recovered by the party prevailing in an action to enforce that contract (e.g., an action "on a contract"). (Id. at 993.) Thus, a broadly worded contractual fee clause may apply to noncontract claims arising out of contract, such as the disgorgement, conversion, and fraudulent concealment causes of action asserted by CDC here. (See, e.g., Xuereb v. Marcus & Millichap, Inc. (1992) 3 Cal.App.4th 1338, 1342 ["under proper circumstances attorney fees may be awarded pursuant to section 1021 in a tort action."]; Maynard v. BTI Group, Inc., 216 Cal.App.4th at 992-93 [under terms of attorney fee provision entitling prevailing party in "any dispute" to attorney fees, plaintiff was the prevailing party although she recovered on a tort theory and did not recover under her cause of action for breach of contract].) Here, the construction contract required Webcor to maintain a contractor's license when it performed work under that contract. (Contract § 13.11 ["So long as Contractor is obligated to render any services to Owner under this Contract, and in any event until completion by Contractor of all Work, Contractor shall obtain and maintain in full force and effect . . . such professional or business licenses . . . necessary to perform Work hereunder in the states in which Contractor's principal place of business and the Project are located."].) At the heart of Plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC's action against Webcor was that Webcor had allegedly breached that contractual obligation, and the action therefore was commenced "because of" Webcor's alleged breach of the construction contract. (See, e.g., Complaint 13, 27, 28; First Amended Complaint 15; Third Amended Complaint 31, 33 ["The contracting party was not licensed when it executed the contract for the construction of the Hotel and performed the work."].) CDC sought disgorgement of "all compensation it paid to the contracting party for the construction of the Hotel in the amount of $144,383,995"-i.e., the full contract price under the construction contract. (TAC 39, 47.) Further, and contrary to its current position, CDC repeatedly admitted in briefing before this Court that its causes of action against Webcor arose out of Webcor's purported "contractual obligations." (end of tenative ruling part 1 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Tax Costs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, March 6, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFFS CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC'S Motion To Tax Costs Plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC's motion to tax costs is denied. Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P.'s entitlement to costs is governed by the parties' agreement, not by Code of Civil Procedure section 1033.5. The attorneys' fees provision in the underlying construction contract entitles Webcor to recover "the actual costs and expenses incurred in connection with the prosecution or defense of such action and any appeals in connection therewith, including actual attorneys' fees, expert fees, consultant's fees or costs and compensation for their time spent by in-house costs." " '[W]hile it is reasonable to interpret a general contractual cost provision by reference to an established statutory definition of costs, we do not discern any legislative intent to prevent sophisticated parties from freely choosing a broader standard authorizing recovery of reasonable litigation charges and expenses.' " (Thrifty Payless, Inc. v. Mariners Mile Gateway, LLC (2010) 185 Cal.App.4th 1050, 1065-1067 [affirming award of expert witness fees pursuant to lease through normal procedures for requesting and taxing costs].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Webcor is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, March 6, 2019, Line 1 - DEFENDANTs SALLY LIU and JOHN LIU's Motion To Compel Further Responses; Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Peter Vestal, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part as follows: (1) all plaintiffs are ordered to answer form interrogatory No. 2.8; (2) plaintiffs Monica Medina and Brian Rangel shall additionally answer form interrogatory No. 2.7; (3) the motion is denied as to form interrogatories No. 2.3 & 2.6; defendants/cross-defendants may obtain the information requested by No. 2.3 & 2.6 by other means should further developments in the case sufficiently alter the current balancing of the parties' respective privacy and discovery rights; and (4) the request for sanctions is denied. rulings.Case Title: THOMPSON BUILDERS CORPORATION FDBA WEST BAY VS. SF TOWNSEND CONDOS, LLC A CALIFORNIA LIMITED ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Of General Reference And Appointment Of Referee
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, March 6, 2019, Line 7. DEFENDANT SF TOWNSEND CONDOS, LLC,'S Motion For Order Of General Reference And Appointment Of Referee Hearing required. The Court believes that it may make more sense to address the reference issue once the transfer and coordination issues are settled. This is especially true because it appears that many of the subcontractors in the Contra Costa actions have yet to appear. (McInerney Decl. 12.) Parties to appear and explain the status of the transfer/coordination efforts and whether the transfer/coordination issue is to be addressed at the 3/25/19 CMC in Thompson Builders Corp. v. Lafayette Town Center, CIVMSC18-01590. =(302/EPS)
